title: Baby Egrets With Punk Rock Hair Win Big Photo Prize!
summary:
Have you ever seen a bird with a mohawk? Well, three baby egrets just won a huge photography contest because their feathers look exactly like punk rock hairstyles! The photo is called "The Punk Rockers," and it took the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual contest.

The photographer is Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian (an animal doctor). She took the picture at a rookery (a bird breeding ground) in Ocean City, New Jersey. The tiny chicks, each no bigger than a golf ball when curled up, were fighting and playing under a ramp. "They were squabbling nonstop," Burke said. Their parents' long legs and big feet loom in the background, making the babies look even smaller.

The contest got more than 17,000 entries from 108 countries! Other winners include a woman praying with oil lamps during a Hindu festival in India, a 14-year-old Navajo girl's coming-of-age ceremony with her horse, a colorful chameleon in South Africa, and a huge circular building in China. Burke started photography in 2014, swapping her binoculars for a camera. "We need to save species like this and save moments like this," she said.
why_it_matters: This story shows that even a silly bird photo can remind us to care for nature and protect animals we share the planet with.

keywords.explanation: A style of music and fashion with spiky hair and bold looks
White birds with long legs, part of the heron family
A magazine published by the Smithsonian Institution in Washington, D.C.
A place where birds gather to breed and raise chicks
Fighting or arguing in a noisy way

questions.question: Who took the winning photo of the baby egrets?
What are the baby egrets' feathers compared to?
How many entries did the contest receive?
Why does Burke take photos of birds?
options: To save memories and protect nature
questions.correct_answer: To save memories and protect nature

background_read: Photography contests like this one celebrate amazing pictures of animals and people from around the world.
The Smithsonian is a group of museums in Washington, D.C., and has been running this contest for 23 years.
Egrets are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act in the United States.
Article_Structure: WHO: Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian from Pennsylvania
WHAT: She photographed baby egrets with punk-rock-like feathers and won Smithsonian Magazine's grand prize
WHERE: At a rookery (bird breeding ground) in Ocean City, New Jersey
WHY: The shot captured the chicks squabbling under a ramp, with parent birds looming large in the background

perspectives.description: The photographer is happy to share her love of birds and hopes people will want to protect them.
Some might argue that calling birds punk rock is just a trend and doesn't really help conservation.
The contest highlights many cultures and animals from around the world, not just one species.
